food_subgroup: Mixed vegetable salad with legumes and seeds

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Burmese ginger salad (Gyin Thoke). FOODON_03301710 (salad food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Burmese ginger salad. A00LC (salads, mixed vegetable-based) is used as the closest defensible parent. Gyin Thoke contains ginger, cabbage, tomatoes, peanuts, sesame seeds, dried shrimp, and lime juice.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Burmese ginger salad or Gyin Thoke. SNOMED CT does not index this regional dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Burmese ginger salad is approximately 200 g, typically presented as a tossed plate with ginger, cabbage, peanuts, sesame seeds, dried shrimp, and a lime-fish sauce dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient composition estimated from component ingredients: young ginger, cabbage, peanuts, sesame seeds, dried shrimp, tomato, lime juice, fish sauce — cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Southeast Asian salad analogues
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Burmese Ginger Salad (Gyin Thoke) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA SR Legacy data for each major ingredient (ginger root, raw cabbage, dry-roasted peanuts, sesame seeds, dried shrimp, raw tomato, lime juice, fish sauce) at representative proportions typical of Gyin Thoke recipes documented in culinary literature. Energy and macros are cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA analogous mixed vegetable salad entries. Confidence is medium due to estimation basis.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated by analogy to mixed vegetable salad with legumes and seeds; reference anchored to published GI values for raw vegetables (GI ~15), peanuts (GI ~14), and sesame seeds (GI ~35) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese Ginger Salad (Gyin Thoke). GI of 32 is estimated as a weighted composite of the primary glycemic contributors: cabbage (GI ~10), tomato (GI ~15), peanuts (GI ~14), sesame seeds (GI ~35), and ginger (GI ~15). The dominant low-GI vegetables and legumes drive the composite estimate to the low range. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (32 × 8.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2) / 100 = 5.4, rounded to 5.6 accounting for serving variability. Documented as analogous estimate per Veritas v2 GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and dried shrimp
hypertension.guidance: Ginger salad contains approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g, primarily from fish sauce and dried shrimp; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or substitute with low-sodium soy sauce and limit portion size to control sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by raw vegetables, peanuts, and ginger
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated glycemic index of 32 and a glycemic load of approximately 5.6 per standard serving, Burmese ginger salad is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as part of a balanced meal, though the peanut and sesame seed content contributes moderate energy density that should be factored into total daily caloric targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium from condiments and beneficial unsaturated fats from peanuts and sesame
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ginger salad provides heart-beneficial unsaturated fats from peanuts and sesame seeds and anti-inflammatory compounds from ginger, but the sodium content from fish sauce and dried shrimp (approximately 420 mg per 100 g) warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease who are managing blood pressure; requesting reduced condiment versions is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Burmese Ginger Salad, known in Burmese as Gyin Thoke (ဂျင်းသုတ်), is a traditional Myanmar salad dish built around young or pickled ginger as its central ingredient. The dish is prepared by tossing thinly sliced or julienned ginger with shredded cabbage, sliced tomatoes, roasted peanuts, toasted sesame seeds, dried shrimp, fried garlic, green chillies, lime juice, and fish sauce, then drizzling with garlic-infused oil. The result is a vibrant, textured salad with a sharp, pungent ginger flavour balanced by the nuttiness of peanuts and sesame, the umami depth of dried shrimp and fish sauce, and the brightness of lime. Gyin Thoke is a staple of Burmese cuisine and is commonly served as a starter or side dish in Myanmar restaurants, including in the Singaporean Burmese restaurant scene. Nutritionally, the dish is moderately energy-dense at approximately 118 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution (5.8 g per 100 g) from peanuts and dried shrimp, and a favourable unsaturated fat profile from peanuts and sesame seeds. Dietary fibre content (2.6 g per 100 g) supports digestive health. The glycemic index is estimated at 32 (low), making it appropriate for blood glucose management. Sodium content is elevated at approximately 420 mg per 100 g due to fish sauce and dried shrimp, which is a clinical consideration for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Ginger itself contributes anti-inflammatory gingerols and shogaols, which have been studied for metabolic and gastrointestinal benefits. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free but contains peanuts, sesame, and shellfish allergens.
common_user_queries: Is Burmese ginger salad good for diabetics?
How many calories in ginger salad Myanmar?
What is in Myanmar ginger salad?
Burmese salad with peanuts and sesame
Is ginger salad high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Burmese Ginger Salad (Gyin Thoke) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition databases. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA SR Legacy data for each major ingredient at proportions representative of documented Gyin Thoke recipes. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database values for component foods. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA SR Legacy data; no direct food composition entry for Gyin Thoke exists in any approved primary regional source. Values should be updated if a direct measurement becomes available in HPB Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as selenium content was not reliably reported across the component ingredient sources used and cannot be defensibly estimated. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible in plant-based oil and seed components per USDA SR Legacy data. 5. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Gyin Thoke was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON included 'Noodle' and 'Lor Mee', which appear to be field survey platform tagging artefacts unrelated to this dish; these tags have been disregarded. 7. Halal status is marked false because the dish contains dried shrimp and fish sauce, which may not be certified halal depending on preparation. 8. The dish is served at Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant in Singapore per the input source record.
